1: Josiah is eight years old at accession. As a type, the young king who will prove to be the last great faithful Davidic ruler before the exile is a foreshadowing of the one who, as a twelve-year-old, was already “about his Father’s business” in the temple (Luke 2:49). Both are young men whose commitment to YHWH is evident before the world expects it.
2: “He did what was right in the sight of the LORD, walking in the ways of his ancestor David and not turning aside to the right or to the left.” The Deuteronomic formula of perfect obedience (“not turning aside to right or left”) is used of the ideal Deuteronomic king (Deuteronomy 17:20; Joshua 1:7). No Davidic king fully embodies this ideal — the formula describes what they should be. Christ perfectly embodies it: “He committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth” (1 Peter 2:22; Isaiah 53:9).
3: “In the eighth year of his reign, while still young, he began to seek (dāraš) the God of his ancestor David.” As a type, the young king beginning to seek God before the visible reform starts anticipates the boy Jesus in the temple: the divine Son already occupied with his Father’s house before his public ministry (Luke 2:41-52). Both begin their decisive engagement with God’s purposes at a young age. The dāraš (seeking) that the Chronicler tracks through the Davidic kings finds its ultimate expression in Christ, who is both the seeker and the one sought (Luke 19:10: “The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost”).
4: The demolition of Baal altars in Josiah’s presence — the king personally overseeing the destruction of false worship. As a type, the anointed king driving out idolatry from the covenant people’s land anticipates Christ’s temple cleansing (John 2:13-17; Matthew 21:12-13): “Zeal for your house has consumed me” (John 2:17, quoting Psalms 69:9). The holy zeal of the anointed king against that which defiles the Father’s house runs from Josiah to Christ.
5: Burning the bones of idolatrous priests on their own altars permanently defiles those altars against future use. As a type, the radical, irreversible dismantling of the cult apparatus by the faithful king points to Christ’s own irreversible act: his once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 10:12-14) permanently dismantles the need for any further sacrifice. “Where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer any offering for sin” (Hebrews 10:18).
6: The reform extends into the northern territories. As a theme, the pan-Israelite scope of Josiah’s reform anticipates the eschatological reunion of all Israel that the prophets project: “I will take the stick of Joseph… and join it with the stick of Judah” (Ezekiel 37:19). Christ’s mission is explicitly pan-Israelite before it extends to the Gentiles: “I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel” (Matthew 15:24), and Paul sees in Christ the “one new man in place of the two” (Ephesians 2:15).
7: The comprehensive vocabulary of destruction applied to every idol — cut down, ground to powder, demolished throughout all Israel. As a shadow, the total annihilation of the idol-cult by the faithful king prefigures Christ’s final destruction of every anti-God power: “He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death” (1 Corinthians 15:25-26). The comprehensiveness of Josiah’s reform is the OT shadow of the comprehensiveness of Christ’s eschatological victory.
8: Josiah initiates the temple repair in his eighteenth year. As a theme, the king who purges the land and then turns to repair the house of God establishes the sequence that matters: external idolatry is addressed before the internal sanctuary. As a revelation of God, this sequence points to Christ’s order of priorities: the temple must be cleansed (John 2) before the temple can become what it was meant to be — a house of prayer for all nations (Mark 11:17).
9: The temple-tax money gathered from Manasseh, Ephraim, all the remnant of Israel, and all Judah and Benjamin. As a theme, the pan-Israelite contribution to the temple repair represents a renewed covenant solidarity: scattered Israel gathering its resources around the house of YHWH. This anticipates the NT image of the church as a spiritual house built from living stones “from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9) — the true pan-Israelite temple that Christ builds.
10: The money entrusted to workmen who oversee the LORD’s house, specifically those “who work faithfully.” As a theme, faithful stewardship of resources entrusted for the building of God’s house is the OT instance of the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14-30): those who work faithfully with what has been entrusted are praised; the house is built by faithful labor with the Master’s resources.
11: “To provide for the buildings that the kings of Judah had neglected.” As a theme, the restoration of what prior unfaithfulness had allowed to decay is the work of the faithful king. Christ comes to restore what the long history of human unfaithfulness has broken: “I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten” (Joel 2:25). Every act of temple repair in the OT is a shadow of the ultimate restoration that Christ brings.
12: The Levites faithfully supervise the construction work. As a theme, the proper Levitical oversight of the temple economy — the priestly intermediaries ensuring the work is done rightly — is the OT form of what the NT describes as the church’s leadership: those appointed to oversee the building of God’s spiritual house (Ephesians 4:11-12), equipping the saints for the work of ministry so that the body is built up.
13: Levites serve as supervisors of burden-bearers and directors of all workers, with scribes and officials and gatekeepers also involved. As a theme, the organized administrative structure of the temple-building project reflects the divine order of the covenant community. Paul’s description of the body with many members each functioning in their role (1 Corinthians 12:12-27) is the NT form of this cooperative building structure under divine oversight.
14: “Hilkiah the priest found the book of the law of the LORD given through Moses.” As a shadow pointing toward Christ, the discovery of the lost Word of God in the temple is one of the most theologically freighted events in the OT. The Word was present all along but unrecognized, housed in the physical structure of the temple. John 1:14 — “The Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen — tabernacled) among us” — is the NT fulfillment: the Word is no longer a scroll hidden in the temple but the living Son present among his people. The book that was found in the house of God becomes the God who dwells in the house of his people.
15: Hilkiah’s announcement — “I have found the book of the law.” As a theme, the finding of the lost Word that transforms a kingdom resonates with the parables of the lost sheep, lost coin, and lost son (Luke 15): what was lost is found, and joy follows. The nation’s response to the found book (tearing clothes, seeking the LORD) is the appropriate response to finding the Word — the same response that genuine hearing of the gospel produces.
16: Shaphan brings the book to the king and reads it aloud. As a theme, the reading of the Word of God before the king — the representative of the covenant people — is the public proclamation of the law that the Deuteronomic king was supposed to read daily (Deuteronomy 17:18-19). The reading that produces transformation is what James calls being “doers of the word and not hearers only” (James 1:22): Josiah hears and acts. This is precisely the posture toward the Word that Christ calls his hearers to (Luke 8:21: “My mother and brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it”).
17: The accountable reporting of the money collected and distributed. As a theme, the transparent stewardship of resources in the service of God’s house reflects the accountability that Christ builds into his kingdom: “From everyone who has been given much, much will be required” (Luke 12:48). The accounting of resources for the temple is a shadow of the final accounting before the one who knows all stewardship.
18: Shaphan reads the book before the king. As a revelation of God, the moment when the Word of God is read and received by the covenant people’s representative is a salvific moment. Christ himself reads the scripture in the Nazareth synagogue and declares, “Today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing” (Luke 4:21): the Word of God read and the Word incarnate speaking are the same Word, now present in person.
19: “When the king heard the words of the law, he tore his clothes.” As a type, the king whose heart is broken by the Word of God contrasts sharply with Jehoiakim (Jeremiah 36:23-24) who cuts the scroll and throws it in the fire. Josiah’s torn clothes are the physical enactment of the “broken and contrite heart” that God does not despise (Psalms 51:17). Christ himself is moved to tears at the hardness of Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) — the opposite of Jehoiakim, the anti-Josiah.
20: The delegation sent to inquire of the LORD. As a theme, the king who does not presume to interpret the Word on his own but seeks prophetic guidance reflects the epistemic humility that Christ commends: “Apart from me you can do nothing” (John 15:5). Josiah has the text but seeks the living prophetic voice that interprets it. The NT equivalent is the Spirit who “will guide you into all the truth” (John 16:13).
21: “Great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out on us because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD.” Josiah’s diagnosis is precise: the accumulated debt of covenant unfaithfulness has created a judicial situation that must be addressed. As a revelation of God, this verse captures the diagnosis of the human condition that the gospel addresses: “The wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness” (Romans 1:18). Christ comes to bear this wrath on behalf of those who cannot bear it themselves.
22: The delegation goes to Huldah the prophetess. As a revelation of God, the fact that YHWH’s word comes through a woman prophet — when male prophets are also available — establishes the principle that prophetic authority is not determined by gender but by YHWH’s choice. In the new covenant, the Spirit is poured out on “your sons and your daughters” (Acts 2:17), removing the gender restriction on prophetic witness. Huldah is an OT anticipation of this new-covenant reality.
23: “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel.” The messenger formula establishing Huldah’s prophetic authority is the same formula used by every true prophet from Moses to Malachi. As a revelation of God, the consistent use of this formula across the OT establishes the coherence of the prophetic word: one God speaking through many voices. Christ fulfills this by being not merely the messenger but the message: “In many and various ways God spoke… but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son” (Hebrews 1:1-2).
24: “I will bring disaster upon this place and upon its inhabitants — all the curses written in the book.” The Deuteronomic curse-fulfillment mechanism: the covenantal consequences of disobedience, written in advance, are now activated. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes that divine judgment is not arbitrary but covenantal: it follows the terms announced at Sinai. Christ bears precisely these curses on behalf of his people: “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us” (Galatians 3:13).
25: “Because they have abandoned me and made offerings to other gods… my wrath will be poured out on this place and will not be quenched.” The unquenchable wrath corresponds to the unquenchable fire of Gehenna that Jesus uses as the image of final judgment (Mark 9:43). The wrath that will not be quenched in Jerusalem’s history (fulfilled in 586 BCE) is the OT form of what the NT presents as the eschatological wrath from which Christ saves those who believe (Romans 5:9; 1 Thessalonians 1:10).
26: The second oracle — addressed to Josiah personally — distinguishes corporate judgment from individual mercy. As a revelation of God, this structural distinction in Huldah’s two-part oracle establishes a principle that Christ himself articulates: the judgment on the nation (Jerusalem will fall) and the mercy for the believing individual (those who flee to the mountains will be saved, Matthew 24:16-18) are both real and must not be collapsed into each other.
27: “Because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before God when you heard his words against this place and its inhabitants, and you humbled yourself before me and tore your clothes and wept before me, I have heard you.” The hearing of YHWH follows the tearing of the heart. As a revelation of God, this verse is a condensed version of the gospel: the broken and contrite heart does not go unheard. Christ himself “offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears” and “was heard because of his reverence” (Hebrews 5:7). The tender-hearted king who weeps before God finds YHWH listening — and so does the High Priest who weeps in Gethsemane.
28: “Your eyes shall not see all the disaster that I will bring upon this place.” Josiah is spared the sight of Jerusalem’s fall by a merciful death. As a type, this mercy-within-judgment echoes Simeon’s blessing: “Lord, now you let your servant depart in peace, for my eyes have seen your salvation” (Luke 2:29-30). Both are faithful covenant servants granted peace in their death before the judgment falls. The difference: Simeon has seen the salvation that Josiah only hoped for.
29: Josiah summons the elders of Judah and Jerusalem for the covenant renewal. As a type, the anointed king assembling the whole people before the Word of God for covenant renewal anticipates the Last Supper: Christ assembling his disciples to establish the new covenant in his blood (Matthew 26:26-28). Both are the king’s covenant assembly at a pivotal moment before his own death.
30: The reading of the covenant book “in the hearing” of the whole assembly — men, women, children, priests, Levites. As a type, the public reading of the Word before the covenant people assembled anticipates Paul’s instructions: “When this letter has been read among you, have it also read in the church of the Laodiceans” (Colossians 4:16). The hearing of God’s word by the entire gathered community is the constitutive act of the covenant people from Sinai through Josiah to the church.
31: “The king stood in his place and made a covenant before the LORD, to follow the LORD and keep his commandments… with all his heart and all his soul.” The full Shema-love formula in a covenant-making context. As a type, the Davidic king making covenant with YHWH on behalf of the people is the pattern that Christ fulfills: he is the covenant himself (Isaiah 42:6: “I will give you as a covenant for the people”), the one who gives his life as the covenant-sealing sacrifice (Hebrews 9:15: “he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance”).
32: Josiah requires all who were present to stand to the covenant. As a theme, the corporate binding to the covenant by the faithful king on behalf of the whole people anticipates the work of Christ as the one who establishes the new covenant obligation on behalf of all who are in him. In Christ, the covenant is no longer merely imposed from outside but fulfilled from within by the one who “through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God” (Hebrews 9:14).
33: “Josiah removed all the abominations from all the territories belonging to the people of Israel and required all who were present in Israel to serve the LORD their God. All his days they did not turn aside from following the LORD.” The Chronicler’s closing evaluation of Josiah is unqualified: under this king, the covenant was fully kept. This is the highest point of the Davidic dynasty before its fall. As a type, Josiah’s reign of total covenant faithfulness is the OT’s closest anticipation of what Christ permanently establishes: the eternal kingdom in which the people of God “will serve him day and night in his temple” (Revelation 7:15), never again turning aside from following the LORD their God.
